{} Metaprogramming · Elixir Contents Cheatsheet
{ } A BookBank field guide

Metaprogramming Elixir

Macros are just functions that take code as data, reshape it, and hand code back to the compiler. This book builds that idea from first principles — and then shows why a language whose programs can read and rewrite themselves turned out to be a quietly perfect fit for the LLM-agentic era.

narrated by The Clear Mentor · example-first, gotchas called out

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

Most languages draw a hard wall between the program and the compiler. Elixir doesn't. Here, your code is made of the same tuples and lists you manipulate every day — so you can grab a chunk of a program, treat it as data, transform it, and feed it back. That's the whole game, and it's less mysterious than it sounds.

Chris McCord's Metaprogramming Elixir taught a generation of us the moves. This book stands on that foundation but asks a 2026 question: now that LLM agents write, call, and patch our code, what does macro-power buy a modern Elixir developer? The answer threads through every chapter — declarations an agent can read, schemas that bound a model's output, and tools like Igniter that let an agent edit your AST without ever touching a regex.

🧭
How to read this

Chapters 1–6 build the machine: code-as-data, quote/unquote, macros, hygiene, the use pattern, and generating code from data. Chapters 7–10 are the payoff: DSLs, metaprogramming in an LLM toolchain, agent-driven code generation, and — crucially — when not to reach for a macro.

cover ·

Table of Contents

  1. Code Is Data Homoiconicity, the AST, and the three-element tuple every Elixir program is made of. the idea
  2. Quote, Unquote & bind_quoted Lift code into data, pour values back in — and the one option that saves you from double-evaluation. the tools
  3. Your First Macro defmacro, require, and the mental model: macros run at compile time and return AST. hands-on
  4. Hygiene & the Caller's Context Why your macro's variables can't clobber the caller's — and how to bend that on purpose with var!. safety
  5. use, __using__ & Compile-Time Hooks The injection pattern behind Ecto, Phoenix, and ExUnit: module attributes, accumulation, @before_compile. the pattern
  6. Generating Functions from Data Loop over a list at compile time and emit a function for each row. Tables become fast pattern-matched code. code-gen
  7. DSLs: The Big Payoff Why schema do … end and get "/" do … end read like mini-languages — and where Spark & Ash take it. declarative
  8. Metaprogramming Meets the LLM Tool catalogs as data, Ecto schemas as structured-output contracts — declarations a model can read and call. LLM
  9. Code That Writes Code, for Agents Igniter & Sourceror patch the AST, not the text; usage_rules and Tidewave/MCP feed agents real context. tooling
  10. With Great Power The official anti-patterns, the first rule of macro club, and a checklist for when a plain function wins. judgment
cover ·
🔑
About this book

Researched from the official Elixir guides (Quote and unquote, Macros, the Macro and Module docs, and the Meta-programming anti-patterns reference) and verified against modern Elixir (1.18 / 1.19). Code is idiomatic and runnable. The agentic chapters draw on real libraries — LangChain for Elixir, Ash AI, Instructor, Jido, and Igniter. Everything renders offline.

🧑‍🏫
A note on the voice

I'll teach the way I'd pair with you on a real change: lead with a runnable example, then explain why it's shaped that way, and stop to flag the foot-guns before they cost you a debugging afternoon. Metaprogramming has more foot-guns than most corners of Elixir — so we'll name every one as we pass it.

code— quote →data (AST)— unquote →code

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.

⌂ Library
